items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: Is genetic modification the same as selective breeding?
items.correct: No — selective breeding chooses parents over generations; genetic modification directly inserts a gene into an organism.
items.misconception: GM crops and selectively bred crops are made the same way.
items.random_wrong: Selective breeding inserts a gene from another species directly.
items.why: Students merge the two; selective breeding relies on natural reproduction, while GM transfers DNA directly, often between different species.

items.q: Does using more antibiotics in farming make animals permanently healthier?
items.correct: No — overusing antibiotics promotes antibiotic-resistant bacteria, which is a serious health risk.
items.misconception: More antibiotics always keep farm animals healthier with no downside.
items.random_wrong: Antibiotics cure viral diseases in farm animals.
items.why: Students see only the immediate benefit, but routine antibiotic use selects for resistant bacteria that can spread to humans.

items.q: Which is NOT a way to increase food security?
items.why: Correct answer: Burning forests for fuel. “Selective breeding” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Producing memory cells. “Killing bacteria directly” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly how many billion is global population in 2026?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
